title: The Ainu
summary: The Ainu are the Indigenous people of Hokkaido, Sakhalin, and the Kuril Islands, whose distinct language, animist religion, and way of life set them apart from the ethnic Japanese who colonized their homeland. For much of the modern era the Japanese state tried to assimilate them out of existence; only in 2008 and 2019 did it formally recognize them as an Indigenous people. They are a living people whose culture is now being revived.
hook: For most of a century, Japanese law had an official word for the Ainu: former aborigines. The people it described had an older, simpler word for themselves. They were ainu — 'human' — the beings of this world, set against the kamuy, the gods who came to visit it. They were the Indigenous people of the north, with their own land, language, and gods. The state that governed them spent decades insisting they were merely Japanese.

beginner.thePeriod:
Long before the northern island was called Hokkaido, the people who lived there had their own name for it. They called it Ainu Mosir, which means the land of humans. The Ainu are the native people of that land. Their home once stretched across Hokkaido, the island of Sakhalin, and the Kuril Islands.

The Ainu are not the same people as the ethnic Japanese. Their ancestors had lived in the north for thousands of years, going back to Japan's ancient hunter-gatherers. The Ainu had their own language, their own gods, and their own way of life. By about seven hundred years ago, the culture we now call Ainu had taken clear shape.

They were hunters, fishers, and traders. Salmon filled their rivers each autumn. Deer and bear lived in their forests. This land, cold and rich, shaped everything about them.
beginner.theBoundaries:
For a long time the Ainu traded with the Japanese to their south, whom they called Wajin. They swapped furs, dried fish, and eagle feathers for iron tools, rice, and lacquer. But the trade did not stay fair.

In time a Japanese lordship, the Matsumae domain, won the sole right to trade with the Ainu. It set the prices, and it set them low. The Ainu grew poorer and angrier. In 1669 an Ainu chief named Shakushain led many groups in a great revolt. The Matsumae crushed it and killed him at a feast that was meant to make peace.

After that the Ainu slipped further under Japanese control. Merchants ran harsh fishing camps and worked Ainu people hard. The old freedom of Ainu Mosir was slowly draining away, long before Japan took the land for good.
beginner.theConditions:
The Ainu believed the world was full of spirits called kamuy. A kamuy could be an animal, a plant, a fire, or a storm. To the Ainu, a bear was not just a bear. It was a mountain god who had put on fur and come to visit, bringing meat as a gift.

Because of this, the Ainu held a ceremony called iomante, the bear-sending. A village would raise a bear cub with great care, almost like a child. After a year or two, the people held a solemn feast. They thanked the bear, then sent its spirit home to the land of the gods.

The Ainu did not use writing. Instead they kept long story-poems called yukar. Elders sang them by the fire. These songs held the memory of gods, heroes, and the making of the world.
beginner.internalDiversity:
The Ainu were never one single group. Ainu on Hokkaido, on Sakhalin, and on the Kuril Islands lived in different ways and spoke different forms of the language. Their culture had also mixed older northern peoples together over many centuries.

Ainu women once marked their identity on their own bodies. They wore dark tattoos around the mouth and on the arms, a sign of beauty and adulthood. Men grew full beards. Both wore robes with bold, curling patterns sewn on to guard against evil.

Life ran with the seasons. In spring and summer, people fished and gathered plants. In autumn they took salmon. In winter they hunted deer and bear in the snow. Villages sat by rivers, each with its own leaders and its own share of the hunting grounds.
beginner.longConsequences:
In 1869 Japan's new government renamed the land Hokkaido and claimed it fully. Settlers poured in. In 1899 a law called the Ainu former aborigines. It took their hunting and fishing grounds, banned their customs, and pushed their children to speak only Japanese.

The damage was deep. The Ainu language almost died. Many Ainu hid who they were to escape being scorned. For a while it seemed the culture might vanish.

But it did not. Ainu people fought back through courts, art, and politics. In 1997 a new law finally began to support Ainu culture. In 2008 Japan's parliament called the Ainu an Indigenous people. A stronger law followed in 2019. In 2020 a national Ainu museum, Upopoy, opened in Hokkaido. The Ainu are still here.
beginner.periodizationDebate:
Who is Ainu today? The question is harder than it sounds. After a century of hiding and mixing, many people with Ainu roots do not say so in public. Official counts find about thirteen thousand Ainu in Hokkaido. The real number is surely much larger, but no one knows it.

There is also a debate about how the Ainu should be remembered. Some worry that Japan now shows off Ainu dance and craft as a colorful attraction, while doing less about land, rights, and past wrongs. A bright museum is not the same as justice.

The deepest point is simple. For a long time Japan called itself one single people, with the Ainu quietly folded in. The Ainu revival says otherwise. It insists that Japan has always held more than one people inside it.

general.thePeriod:
Each autumn the rivers of Hokkaido filled with salmon, running thick enough to be dried and stored against the long northern winter. Around that seasonal abundance, and the deer and bear of the forests behind it, a distinctive people took shape. The Ainu were the Indigenous inhabitants of Japan's far north, and their ancestry ran deep. Genetic and archaeological evidence links them closely to the Jomon, the ancient foragers of the archipelago. That link is far closer than any connecting them to the ethnic Japanese of the main islands.

The culture recognizable as Ainu emerged fairly late. It crystallized out of the earlier Satsumon culture of Hokkaido around the twelfth and thirteenth centuries, absorbing influences from the maritime Okhotsk culture to the north. What set the Ainu apart was not one trait but a whole world. They spoke a language unrelated to any other, and practiced a religion built around spirits called kamuy. Their economy rested on hunting, fishing, and long-distance trade rather than the wet-rice farming that defined the states to the south.
general.theBoundaries:
For centuries the Ainu were not isolated but deeply enmeshed in trade. They exchanged furs, dried salmon, and eagle feathers, prized for samurai arrows, southward for iron, rice, sake, and lacquerware, and northward with the Asian mainland. This commerce became a trap. In the seventeenth century the Matsumae domain, the Japanese lordship at Hokkaido's southern tip, secured a monopoly over all trade with the Ainu. It used that power to dictate ruinous terms.

The resentment erupted in 1669, when a leader named Shakushain united numerous Ainu groups in a broad uprising against Matsumae authority. The domain suppressed it and had Shakushain killed at a banquet supposedly called to negotiate peace. The defeat marked a turning point. Over the following century the Ainu were drawn ever tighter into a system of contract fisheries and merchant control. It eroded their independence, their subsistence economy, and their numbers, well before the modern Japanese state formally annexed their land.
general.theConditions:
Ainu life rested on a bargain with the spirit world. Nearly everything of consequence, an animal, a tool, a mountain, a disease, was understood as a kamuy, a spiritual being that had entered the human world in a temporary disguise. The most elaborate of the rituals owed to the kamuy was the iomante, the bear-sending. A community reared a captured bear cub for a year or more, then held a solemn ritual to release its spirit. The bear returned to the realm of the gods laden with gifts and gratitude, so that it would come again.

The society that practiced these rites had no writing and no unified state. Knowledge and history passed through oral epics, the yukar, chanted through long evenings. Authority was local, resting with village leaders and the heads of households rather than a king. This was a world organized around rivers and hunting territories, kinship and reciprocity, and a careful sense that the human and spirit worlds owed each other constant exchange.
general.internalDiversity:
Beneath the single label lay real variety. The Hokkaido, Sakhalin, and Kuril Ainu spoke dialects so distinct that speakers could struggle to understand one another, and they adapted to different environments and neighbors. The culture itself was a composite. It blended the inland Satsumon tradition with maritime Okhotsk elements and centuries of contact with Japanese, Nivkh, and other peoples around the northern seas.

Identity was written on the body and the loom. Women received distinctive tattoos around the mouth and on the forearms as they reached adulthood, and both sexes wore robes decorated with bold, swirling appliqué patterns believed to turn away harmful spirits. Daily life followed a seasonal round of fishing, plant-gathering, and hunting. Villages coordinated that work, and each held recognized rights over particular stretches of river and forest. It was a decentralized world, held together by kinship, trade, and shared ritual rather than by any central power.
general.longConsequences:
The Meiji state that emerged after 1868 transformed the Ainu world with startling speed. In 1869 it renamed Ezo as Hokkaido and folded it fully into Japan, opening the island to waves of settlers. The Hokkaido Former Aborigines Protection Act of 1899 completed the process. It seized Ainu hunting and fishing grounds, outlawed core customs, and forced Ainu children into Japanese-language schooling. Assimilation was the explicit goal, and it came close to succeeding. The Ainu language slid toward extinction, and many Ainu concealed their ancestry to escape discrimination.

Yet the culture survived, and from the late twentieth century it revived. Ainu activism, in the courts and in public life, forced a slow reversal of policy. A 1997 law began supporting Ainu culture. In 2008 the Diet formally named the Ainu an Indigenous people, and a 2019 law enshrined that status while funding cultural programs. In 2020 the national museum and park complex Upopoy opened at Shiraoi, a state monument to a people the state had once tried to erase.
general.periodizationDebate:
The Ainu pose a hard problem of definition, both past and present. Because Japan long imagined itself as a uniquely homogeneous nation, the very existence of an Indigenous people with a separate language and religion was, for decades, officially minimized. Counting the Ainu today is genuinely difficult. Generations of intermarriage, assimilation, and hidden identity have muddied the record. Official surveys record around thirteen thousand people in Hokkaido, while the number with some Ainu ancestry is unknown and probably far higher.

A sharper debate surrounds what recognition should mean. Since the 2019 law, the Japanese state has invested heavily in Ainu culture as heritage, funding festivals, crafts, and the Upopoy complex. Critics, including many Ainu, argue that this celebrates dance and design while sidestepping harder questions of land, fishing rights, and compensation for past dispossession. The unresolved tension is whether naming the Ainu a distinct people is a first step toward justice or a substitute for it.

advanced.thePeriod:
The analytical difficulty with the Ainu begins with the boundaries of the category itself. The word names both an ancient descent, reaching back through the Satsumon culture to the Jomon foragers, and a historically specific ethnic formation that sharpened precisely as the Japanese state pressed against it. The people did not simply exist and then encounter Japan; the encounter did much to constitute Ainu and Wajin as opposed identities. The dense trade in furs and marine goods that historians such as Brett Walker have reconstructed was not a meeting of two sealed worlds but the very process through which each side came to define the other.

This unsettles the intuitive framing of an aboriginal people invaded from outside. It was aboriginal, and it was invaded; but the ethnic sharpness now read backward across the whole record is partly an artifact of the incorporation that followed. The Ainu are most precisely understood not as a fixed nation awaiting conquest but as a people whose very coherence was forged, and then frozen, in the encounter that dispossessed them.
advanced.theBoundaries:
The conventional image of a free Ainu world ending abruptly with Meiji annexation does not survive close reading of the Tokugawa centuries. Long before 1869, the contract-fishery system, in which Japanese merchants leased the right to exploit designated Ainu districts, had converted much of the population from independent hunters and traders into a coerced labor force tied to commercial fishing. Walker's account presents this as a gradual economic and ecological absorption: the Ainu were pulled into a Japanese-dominated market, and their environment reshaped by it, well before any formal seizure of sovereignty.

The stakes of this reperiodization are real. If dispossession was already far advanced under the shogunate, then the Meiji measures of 1869 and 1899 completed a long process rather than inaugurating one, and the tidy line between a precolonial Ainu Mosir and a colonized Hokkaido dissolves. What looks in the standard account like a sudden colonial rupture was in large part the legal ratification of a subordination that commerce and disease had been building for two centuries.
advanced.theConditions:
Ainu religion has become a site of interpretive temptation. The kamuy system and the iomante are frequently enlisted as evidence of an ecological ethic, a people living in reciprocal balance with nature, and the reading is not baseless: the ceremonies do encode obligations of restraint and return toward the animals that sustained life. But the romantic version flattens a harder reality. The same society that honored the bear as a visiting god also hunted intensively for a market that wanted its pelts, and the trade pressures documented for the Tokugawa period drove exactly the kind of overexploitation the ecological reading imagines the Ainu to have transcended.

A parallel tension runs through the yukar. Their survival owes much to salvage transcription, most famously by the young Ainu writer Chiri Yukie, whose 1923 collection rendered the chants of the gods in both Ainu and Japanese. That act of preservation was also a translation into the categories of the very culture that was displacing Ainu orality, and every later use of the yukar as authentic Ainu voice inherits the complication.
advanced.internalDiversity:
The hardest questions about the Ainu today concern the very ground of identity. After a century in which passing as Wajin was the rational response to stigma, Ainu identity is now often something claimed and rebuilt rather than simply inherited. The anthropologist ann-elise lewallen has described how Ainu women reconstruct indigeneity through practices such as traditional clothwork, treating identity as something actively made. This reframing of Japan as a settler society whose Indigenous population must reassemble itself cuts sharply against the national self-image of homogeneity.

It also generates genuine discomfort about criteria. If official counts capture only those willing to declare an identity that still carries social cost, then every population figure is a political artifact, not a demographic fact. And if indigeneity is partly self-crafted, the state and the courts are left adjudicating authenticity, deciding whose ceremony, whose language class, whose ancestry qualifies, a role that reproduces, in gentler form, the same power to define the Ainu from outside that the assimilation era wielded so bluntly.
advanced.longConsequences:
What matters about the settlement reached between 1997 and 2019 is the legal machinery that produced it, because the machinery determines the ceiling. When the Sapporo District Court decided the Nibutani Dam case in March 1997, ruling the expropriation of Kayano Shigeru's land unlawful, it grounded the holding in a right to enjoy one's own culture drawn from Article 27 of the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights and Article 13 of the constitution. Indigeneity thereby entered Japanese law as a cultural interest to be balanced against public benefit, not as a political status generating claims of its own. The court promptly performed that balance and let the nearly finished dam stand. The plaintiffs won the doctrine and lost the river, which is a fair description of everything that followed.

The Ainu Cultural Promotion Act passed months later, and the 2019 law extended the same logic: heritage funding granted, self-determination, restored resource rights, apology, and compensation all withheld. Upopoy is that logic in architecture, recognition staged in the one register the settlement will pay for. The cost surfaced in April 2024, when the Sapporo District Court heard the Raporo Ainu Nation's claim to commercial salmon fishing near the mouth of the Tokachi River. The court accepted that Ainu there had taken salmon since the seventeenth century and held a right to continue that culture, then found the river public property and denied that indigeneity carried any inherent commercial right. The scholarly argument is over what the cultural frame is: a ratchet that later claims can widen, or a ceiling that converts every material demand into an application for festival funding. Raporo is the strongest evidence yet for the second reading, and it measures the distance between the Japanese settlement and the treaty claims and land returns won by Indigenous movements elsewhere.
advanced.periodizationDebate:
The deepest problem is whose categories organize the account. Almost the entire documentary record of the Ainu before the twentieth century was produced by outsiders: Matsumae officials, merchants, explorers, and later ethnographers who studied the Ainu as a vanishing race, collecting skulls and photographs even as state policy hastened the vanishing. To write Ainu history from these sources is to see the Ainu largely through the eyes of those dispossessing them, and the salvage impulse that preserved so much, the transcribed epics, the museum collections, was entangled with the assumption that the living people were finished.

The contemporary revival inverts the premise but cannot fully escape the archive. When Ainu institutions reclaim the yukar or rebuild a ceremony, they work with materials shaped by that outside gaze, and even the framework of an Indigenous people, drawn from international rights law, is an external template laid over a decentralized society that never conceived of itself as a single nation. The most rigorous recent work accepts this bind rather than pretending to transcend it, reading the sources against their grain to recover Ainu agency while conceding that a wholly Ainu-authored past is, for the deep record, unrecoverable.

foundation.theStory: The Ainu pose a problem many nations would rather not face: what happens to a people when a modern state decides they should not exist as a separate people at all? Here is an Indigenous people of the north, with a language related to no other, a religion of spirits and bear ceremonies, and an economy of hunting and trade, that the Japanese state spent roughly a century trying to dissolve into itself. Understanding the Ainu means tracing both that attempted erasure and the stubborn survival that followed it.
foundation.theDebate: Historians disagree about when Ainu dispossession really happened. The familiar story blames the Meiji government's annexation of Hokkaido after 1869 and the assimilation law of 1899. But scholars such as Brett Walker argue that the decisive damage came earlier, during the Tokugawa period, when Japanese merchants and the Matsumae domain turned the Ainu trade into a system of monopoly and forced labor. There is a second, present-day debate over recognition: since Japan formally acknowledged the Ainu as Indigenous in 2008 and 2019, is state-funded celebration of Ainu culture a genuine step toward justice, or a way of avoiding the costlier questions of land and rights?
foundation.whyItMatters: The Ainu case opens up several larger subjects: the myth of Japanese ethnic homogeneity, the way trade and debt can subjugate a people before armies ever arrive, and the global pattern by which modern nation-states absorbed the Indigenous societies inside their borders. It also offers a clear, well-documented example of cultural revival, showing how a people can begin to rebuild a language and identity that a state had tried to extinguish. To understand the Ainu is to see Japan, and the process of nation-building itself, from the outside in.
questions: How can a people lose their independence through trade and debt rather than through outright war or conquest?
Why might a government prefer to fund a people's music and crafts rather than return their land or fishing rights?
What does the near-death and revival of the Ainu language show about how languages are lost, and whether they can be brought back?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: English-language scholarship on the Ainu is comparatively small but pointed. Brett Walker's environmental history reframed Ainu subjugation as a long process of ecological and economic absorption under the Tokugawa order, shifting attention away from the Meiji rupture. Richard Siddle traced the modern politics of Ainu identity and resistance, showing how ideas of race were built and contested across the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. More recent work, such as ann-elise lewallen's study of Ainu women and cultural revival, applies the framework of settler colonialism to Japan and treats indigeneity as something actively remade rather than merely inherited. Alongside these outside scholars stand Ainu voices themselves, from Chiri Yukie's 1923 transcriptions to the memoir and activism of figures like Kayano Shigeru, which insist on the Ainu as authors of their own history.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence for Ainu history is powerful but lopsided. Rich material survives: the oral epics, the tools and robes in museum collections, and the archaeological record from Satsumon and Okhotsk sites, alongside detailed written accounts from Japanese officials, merchants, and later ethnographers. The imbalance is that almost all the written sources come from outsiders, often the same people or institutions responsible for the dispossession, and many were compiled under the assumption that the Ainu were a dying race. This shapes what can and cannot be known. The archaeology can establish subsistence, exchange, and settlement; the texts can document policy and trade; but the Ainu understanding of their own world before the twentieth century reaches us mostly through outside filters, and questions of pre-contact population and belief carry real uncertainty.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A defensible account of the Ainu refuses two easy stories. It rejects the nationalist fiction that the Ainu were always simply part of Japan, and it also resists a purely romantic image of ecological harmony shattered by outsiders. The stronger position holds several things at once: that the Ainu are a genuinely distinct Indigenous people with deep roots in the north; that their subjugation was a long economic process, not only a sudden Meiji conquest; and that their religion and trade were real and complex rather than idyllic. On the present controversy, a serious argument has to weigh what recognition has actually delivered. Acknowledging that the 2019 law brought real gains in cultural funding while noting what it withheld, land, resource rights, an apology, is not fence-sitting; it is the position the evidence supports.
questions: If the surviving written record of the Ainu was mostly produced by those who dispossessed them, how should a historian use it without simply repeating their assumptions?
Is the international category of 'Indigenous people' a good fit for a decentralized society that never saw itself as a single nation, and what does applying it reveal or distort?
How should we weigh cultural recognition against material redress when judging whether a state has done justice to an Indigenous people?

research.topic: Ainu descent from the Jomon and the late crystallization of Ainu culture
research.content: Genetic and archaeological evidence solidly supports that the Ainu are the closest living descendants of Japan's Jomon foragers, far more so than the mainland Japanese, who derive most of their ancestry from later Yayoi-period migrants from the Asian continent. It is likewise well established that the specific cultural complex called Ainu emerged relatively late, developing out of Hokkaido's Satsumon culture with Okhotsk-culture influence around the twelfth and thirteenth centuries. These findings are broadly agreed. What remains debated is how sharply an Ainu ethnic identity should be projected back before this formation, and how much continuity to read across the long span from the Jomon.

research.topic: Reading the Ainu experience through settler colonialism and a Tokugawa-era dispossession
research.content: A growing body of scholarship reframes the Ainu experience using the concept of settler colonialism and pushes the decisive moment of dispossession back from the Meiji annexation into the Tokugawa-era contract-fishery system. This work, associated with environmental historians such as Brett Walker and with anthropologists of the contemporary revival such as ann-elise lewallen, treats Ainu incorporation as a long economic and ecological process and Ainu indigeneity as something actively reconstructed. It is reshaping the field but is not yet a settled consensus, and it sits in tension both with older narratives centered on the Meiji state and with Japanese official framings that stress cultural promotion over colonial redress.

research.topic: The Ainu world in its own terms before outside documentation
research.content: We cannot fully recover how the Ainu understood themselves, their society, and their past before the era of sustained Japanese and Western documentation. The evidence that would resolve it, extensive self-authored records from before the twentieth century, cannot exist, because Ainu society was non-literate and transmitted its history orally; the epics and rituals that survive were transcribed late, often by outsiders or under outside influence, and filtered through the categories of a culture already displacing the Ainu. From material remains and the oral tradition we can responsibly infer subsistence patterns, trade networks, and the broad shape of the kamuy religion, and reconstruct much of the language. But pre-contact population size, the precise pre-market character of Ainu belief and land use, and the Ainu's own sense of collective identity before it was defined against Japan must remain substantially unknowable.

comparativeNarrative.name: One people, quietly enlarged
comparativeNarrative.content: For much of the modern era the Japanese state told a story of a single, homogeneous nation, and the Ainu were something to be folded gently into it. In this view, assimilation was progress: the Ainu were being lifted into modern citizenship, their hardship the price of joining a unified country. Today the official frame has shifted to celebration. The Ainu are honored as a rich Indigenous culture, showcased at Upopoy and funded by law. What this account still tends to avoid is the cost side of its own past, the seized land, the banned language, the deaths, and the chance that recognition might demand more than festivals. It turns a history of erasure into a story of harmony.

comparativeNarrative.name: A nation that refused to vanish
comparativeNarrative.content: From within the Ainu community, the story is one of survival against a state that expected them to disappear. Elders remember the shame attached to the language, the pressure to pass as Japanese, the relatives who hid their tattoos and their names. But they also carry the yukar, the ceremonies, and the memory of Ainu Mosir as a homeland taken, not given up. For many Ainu, recent recognition is real but incomplete. It restores pride and funds culture, yet leaves untouched the questions that matter most: the land, the fishing rights, and an honest reckoning with the past. This view insists that the Ainu are not a museum exhibit but a living people with unfinished claims.

comparativeNarrative.name: Japan among settler nations
comparativeNarrative.content: A third vantage sets Japan beside other countries built on Indigenous land. Seen this way, the Ainu story loses its air of national exception and joins a familiar global pattern: a modern state expands into a homeland, absorbs its people through trade and law, suppresses their language, then later offers recognition once the threat of real resistance has passed. This comparison is clarifying, but it can also mislead. Japan's path ran through commerce and gradual incorporation more than through treaties and frontier wars, and its Indigenous population is proportionally tiny. Holding the parallel and the difference together is the point: the Ainu case rhymes with Australia or North America without ever quite repeating them.

comparativeSummary: These three vantages agree on most of the facts and diverge on their meaning. The Japanese national frame reads the Ainu past as absorption softening into celebration; the Ainu view reads it as an attempted erasure survived, with the hardest claims still unmet; the comparative frame reads it as one instance of a worldwide colonial pattern, alike but not identical. Held together, they show that the argument over the Ainu is less about what happened than about what is owed. The deepest disagreement is whether recognizing the Ainu as a distinct people completes a reconciliation or merely begins one.

rabbitHole.label: Tokugawa Japan
rabbitHole.reason: The shogunal order of Tokugawa Japan is the frame within which the Matsumae domain held its monopoly on Ainu trade and the contract-fishery system took shape, the machinery that subjugated the Ainu long before formal annexation.

rabbitHole.label: The Meiji Restoration
rabbitHole.reason: The Meiji state renamed Ezo as Hokkaido in 1869, opened it to mass settlement, and passed the 1899 assimilation law, converting a gradual subjugation into an explicit program of erasing the Ainu as a distinct people.

rabbitHole.label: Colonialism
rabbitHole.reason: Recent scholarship reads the Ainu experience as settler colonialism inside Japan: the absorption of a homeland and its Indigenous population by an expanding state, a pattern the entry shares with colonial histories elsewhere.

rabbitHole.label: Nationalism
rabbitHole.reason: The modern myth of a uniquely homogeneous Japanese nation depended on minimizing the Ainu; their existence, and their revival, is a standing challenge to the idea that a state contains only one people.

rabbitHole.label: Shinto
rabbitHole.reason: The Ainu kamuy religion, an animism of spirits inhabiting animals, tools, and forces, offers a sharp contrast to Shinto, which was reorganized into the national faith of the very state that suppressed Ainu belief.

rabbitHole.label: Jomon Japan
rabbitHole.reason: The Ainu are the closest living descendants of the Jomon, the ancient foragers of the archipelago, making Jomon Japan the deep ancestral background from which Ainu culture eventually formed.

rabbitHole.label: The Ryukyu Kingdom
rabbitHole.reason: The Ryukyuans of Okinawa are the other distinct people absorbed at Japan's edge, in the south, and their incorporation and assimilation closely parallel the Ainu story in the north.

rabbitHole.label: Indigenous Australians
rabbitHole.reason: Australia offers a comparable arc of Indigenous dispossession, attempted assimilation, and late, partial recognition in a settler society, sharpening what is common and what is distinctive in the Ainu case.

reference.author: Kayano, Shigeru
reference.title: Our Land Was a Forest: An Ainu Memoir
reference.annotation: Written by an Ainu folklorist, collector, and later member of Japan's Diet, this is a rare book-length account of the Ainu world from the inside, translated into English by Kyoko and Lili Selden. It braids personal memory with the wider dispossession of Ainu land, economy, and language, and its value is precisely that it is an insider's testimony against a record otherwise dominated by outsiders.

reference.author: Walker, Brett L.
reference.title: The Conquest of Ainu Lands: Ecology and Culture in Japanese Expansion, 1590-1800
reference.annotation: The pivotal English-language study of the Tokugawa-era subjugation of the Ainu, arguing that trade, ecology, and disease absorbed them into a Japanese-dominated order well before Meiji annexation. It is the work that reset the chronology of Ainu dispossession and reframed Ezo as a borderland rather than a simple frontier.

reference.author: Siddle, Richard M.
reference.title: Race, Resistance and the Ainu of Japan
reference.annotation: Traces how ideas of race, and Ainu resistance to them, were constructed across the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, connecting the assimilation era to the modern politics of identity and rights. It is the standard treatment of Ainu identity as something made and contested rather than simply inherited.

reference.author: lewallen, ann-elise
reference.title: The Fabric of Indigeneity: Ainu Identity, Gender, and Settler Colonialism in Japan
reference.annotation: An ethnography of the contemporary Ainu revival, centered on how Ainu women rebuild indigeneity through practices such as traditional clothwork. It brings the framework of settler colonialism to bear on Japan and is the sharpest account of identity as an ongoing act of reconstruction rather than a fixed inheritance.

reference.author: Howell, David L.
reference.title: Geographies of Identity in Nineteenth-Century Japan
reference.annotation: Howell shows how everyday markers such as hairstyle, dress, and personal names did the work of sorting Japanese from Ainu under the Tokugawa order, and how the Meiji state converted those status boundaries into ethnic ones. It supplies the mechanism the assimilation story usually leaves implicit: why cutting hair and changing names counted as policy rather than decoration.

commerce.title: Ainu: Spirit of a Northern People
commerce.author: William W. Fitzhugh and Chisato O. Dubreuil (eds.)
commerce.note: The Ainu are shown here before they are explained. Built around a 1999 Smithsonian exhibition, it is lavishly illustrated with robes, tools, and photographs, and its short essays on archaeology, religion, and modern life demand no background at all.

commerce.title: Our Land Was a Forest: An Ainu Memoir
commerce.author: Kayano Shigeru
commerce.note: The book to read once you want an Ainu voice rather than an outsider's summary. Kayano grew up as the culture was being dismantled and spent his life saving it; his memoir carries the loss and the fight to recover it in a way no survey can.

commerce.title: Race, Resistance and the Ainu of Japan
commerce.author: Richard M. Siddle
commerce.note: Turn here to follow how the Ainu became a 'race' in Japanese eyes and how they pushed back. Siddle connects the assimilation laws to today's identity politics, and it is the clearest guide to why recognition became such a fought-over word.

commerce.title: The Conquest of Ainu Lands: Ecology and Culture in Japanese Expansion, 1590-1800
commerce.author: Brett L. Walker
commerce.note: For readers ready to argue about periodization, this is the book that moved the story of Ainu subjugation back into the Tokugawa era. Dense with trade, ecology, and disease, it rewards patience and reshapes how the whole colonization is understood.

commerce.title: The Fabric of Indigeneity: Ainu Identity, Gender, and Settler Colonialism in Japan
commerce.author: ann-elise lewallen
commerce.note: Cloth is the thread lewallen follows — an ethnography of the revival that watches Ainu women remake identity through craft. Demanding, and unusually attentive to how indigeneity is rebuilt day to day, in the living present rather than the deep past.

popularCulture.title: Golden Kamuy (2014, Satoru Noda)
popularCulture.description: This hugely popular manga follows a soldier and a young Ainu girl, Asirpa, hunting for hidden gold in early 1900s Hokkaido. Noda worked with an Ainu language expert, and the story lovingly shows Ainu food, hunting, and belief. It also bends history for adventure, piling on violence and treasure-hunt thrills, so the harder story of Ainu loss sits quietly behind a rollicking tale.

popularCulture.title: Ainu Mosir (2020, Takeshi Fukunaga)
popularCulture.description: This quiet drama follows Kanto, a teenage Ainu boy in a Hokkaido tourist town, pulled between the modern world and his heritage. The director cast real Ainu people, not stars, so the daily texture feels true. The film circles the bear ceremony and a boy's grief. Some may wish it said more about Ainu politics, but its power lies in small, honest moments.

popularCulture.title: Upopoy National Ainu Museum and Park (opened 2020)
popularCulture.description: Upopoy is Japan's first national museum devoted to the Ainu, opened in 2020 beside Lake Poroto in Hokkaido. It shows Ainu clothing, tools, song, and dance, and treats the Ainu as a living people, not a lost one. Many Ainu welcome it. Others note that a bright, state-run museum can warmly display culture while quietly setting aside harder questions about land and rights.

popularCulture.title: The Nibutani Dam Decision (1997)
popularCulture.description: In 1997 a Japanese court ruled on a dam built on Ainu land at Nibutani. For the first time, a court called the Ainu an Indigenous people whose culture the law must respect. Yet the judges let the finished dam stand. The case became a landmark, and a warning, showing that recognition on paper does not always undo harm already done on the ground.

popularCulture.title: Ainu Shin'yoshu (Ainu Songs of Gods) (1923, Chiri Yukie)
popularCulture.description: Chiri Yukie was a young Ainu woman who wrote down the sacred chants, the yukar, that her people had always sung aloud. Her 1923 book set them in both Ainu and Japanese. She died at nineteen, just before it appeared. The collection saved songs that might have been lost, and it stands as a rare record of the Ainu world in an Ainu voice.
